attachment.content:
Entire CLI will link this conversation to your next commit.
  1 other active conversation(s) in this workspace will also be included.
  Use 'entire status' for more information.

- impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Add API key requirement to omlx

content.text: Now the settings UI — an API key field under the oMLX section:

content.content: The file /Users/heath/code/obsidian-ai-expander/src/settingsTab.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +        "Sent as a Bearer token. Required by some oMLX deployments; leave blank for an unauthenticated local server."

content.text: The merge logic in `main.ts` already spreads `omlx` defaults, so existing users get `apiKey: ""` automatically. Let me typecheck the build:

Typechecks clean. Done — oMLX now supports an optional API key:

- **`settings.ts`** — added `apiKey: string` to the `omlx` block and its default (`""`).
- **`providers/omlx.ts`** — constructor accepts `apiKey?`; a `headers()` helper adds `Authorization: Bearer <key>` (only when set) to both `expand()` (streaming chat) and `listModels()`.
- **`settingsTab.ts`** — password-masked "API key" field under the oMLX section.

The key is optional: blank leaves requests unauthenticated, so a plain local server still works, while deployments that require auth now get the Bearer token. Existing users' settings migrate automatically via the spread in `mergeSettings`.

aiTitle: Add API key requirement to omlx

input.command: cat esbuild.config.mjs && echo "--- is cwd a symlink? ---" && ls -ld /Users/heath/code/obsidian-ai-expander && echo "--- dev watcher running? ---" && pgrep -fl esbuild.config.mjs || echo "no esbuild watcher process"
input.description: Check esbuild config, symlink status, watcher

content.text:
The symlink handles **distribution** (your vault loads `main.js` straight from this directory), but it doesn't compile anything. `main.js` is an esbuild bundle of the TypeScript source, and it only regenerates when the `npm run dev` watcher is running — which it currently isn't (no esbuild process, and `main.js` is dated 15:17, before these edits).

So the changes aren't live yet. Let me build:

input.command: npm run build 2>&1 | tail -15 && echo "--- main.js timestamp ---" && ls -la main.js

content.content:
> obsidian-ai-expander@0.1.0 build
> tsc -noEmit -skipLibCheck && node esbuild.config.mjs production

--- main.js timestamp ---
-rw-r--r--@ 1 heath  staff  16172 May 17 16:25 main.js

toolUseResult.stdout:
> obsidian-ai-expander@0.1.0 build
> tsc -noEmit -skipLibCheck && node esbuild.config.mjs production

--- main.js timestamp ---
-rw-r--r--@ 1 heath  staff  16172 May 17 16:25 main.js

content.text:
Built — `main.js` is now freshly bundled (16:25, includes the API key changes).

So to answer directly:

- **Symlink** = your vault loads `main.js` from this folder, so no manual copy. ✅ Automatic.
- **Build** = `main.js` only updates when `npm run dev` is watching or you run `npm run build`. Not automatic right now.

It's built now, so just reload the plugin in Obsidian (toggle it off/on in Community Plugins, or Cmd+R / "Reload app without saving") to pick it up. If you want edits to be truly hands-off going forward, run `npm run dev` in the background — it watches `src/` and rebundles on every save (you'd still reload the plugin in Obsidian to see changes).

content: Added optional API key support to the oMLX provider (settings, provider headers, settings UI) and ran a production build, so main.js is current. Next: reload the plugin in Obsidian to pick up the changes. (disable recaps in /config)
